perturbation.original_text: 3. OBLIGATIONS OF DISTRIBUTOR In exchange for the exclusive distribution right and license provided in Section 2 of this Agreement, Distributor hereby agrees during the Term to use its best commercial efforts to promote, advertise and distribute the Products throughout the Territory, including, without limitation, the following:
perturbation.changed_text: 3. OBLIGATIONS OF DISTRIBUTOR In exchange for the exclusive distribution right and license provided in Section 2 of this Agreement, Distributor agrees to endeavor to promote, advertise, and distribute the Products throughout the Territory, potentially including, but not limited to, the following:
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "use its best commercial efforts" with "endeavor to promote" weakens the obligation. "Endeavor" is vague and doesn't define the required level of effort. The phrase "potentially including, but not limited to" makes the subsequent obligations non-mandatory, creating uncertainty about the Distributor's responsibilities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Ontario Law - implied duty of good faith in contractual performance
perturbation.law_explanation: Ontario law implies a duty of good faith and fair dealing in the performance of contracts. This modification, by weakening the obligation to promote and distribute, allows the distributor to potentially do very little, undermining the purpose of the exclusive distribution agreement and potentially violating the implied duty of good faith. The original text clearly outlines the distributor agreeing to use its 'best commercial efforts' which is an actual commitment, whereas the modified text of 'endeavor' is a vague promise.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: The New Legal Framework For Teleworking In Greece - Employee Rights/ Labour Relations - Greece ARTICLE 29 April 2022 The New Legal Framework For Teleworking In Greece IL Ius Laboris More Contributor Ius Laboris is consistently recognised as the leading legal service provider in employment, immigration and pensions law. Our firms help international employers navigate the world of work successfully. Explore Firm Details During the COVID-19 pandemic, working from home, which was previously very unusual, became common in Greece. Greece Employment and HR Konstantina Botsari (Kremalis) To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. During the COVID-19 pandemic, working from home, which was previously very unusual, became common in Greece. New legal provisions now regulate teleworking, including employers' duties and employees' rights. Traditionally, teleworking in Greece was regulated in article 5 of law L.3846/2010. However, teleworking as an employment practice was not widely implemented until the outbreak of the COVID-19 pandemic. This provision was recently amended by the new Labour Law (4808/2021). Article 67 of the new Labour Law regulates remote employment more comprehensively; it also defines the types of teleworking or remote working and whether these types of work can be implemented unilaterally or not. In addition, it has reinforced employees' entitlements. Specifically, it acknowledges a right to disconnect for teleworkers for the first time, that is, a right to refrain completely from working and communicating digitally or answering telephone calls, emails, or any form of communication outside working hours and during their statutory leave. Additionally, it contains a provision that the monitoring of the employee's performance needs to be conducted in a privacy-friendly manner and in compliance with the protection of personal data. Teleworkers' health and safety is also acknowledged and the employer must inform the teleworker regarding its policy on health and safety at work, including specifications for teleworking areas, the rules on the use of visual display screens, breaks, and organisational and technical means. Finally, the employer has to ensure that teleworkers have the same rights and obligations as employees working on-site in relation to workload, assessment criteria and procedures, rewards, access to information concerning the undertaking and other matters. One of the more significant aspects of the new regulation of teleworking is that the employer bears the costs incurred by the employee as a result of teleworking, that is, the cost of equipment, unless it is agreed that the employee's equipment will be used, telecommunications, maintenance of the equipment and damage repair. The employee must be provided with technical support for the performance of work and the employer undertakes to reimburse the costs of repairing or replacing the equipment as required. This obligation also applies to equipment owned by the employee unless the agreement or the employment relationship stipulates otherwise. In accordance with article 67, a Ministerial Decision (98490/3.12.2021) was issued, determining the minimum monthly cost that the employer is required to pay for teleworking. The compensation covers the use of household space as a workplace amounting to EUR 13 per month, the cost of telecommunications at EUR 10 per month, and the maintenance costs for equipment at EUR 5 per month. The payment by the employer is paid into the employee's payroll account listed as ‘teleworking costs'. If the employee teleworks fewer than 22 days in a month, then the employer has to pay 1/22 of the above expenses for each day of actual teleworking. If the employer has separate contracts with fixed and mobile communication providers and directly pays the cost of telecommunications and if the employer provides the equipment to the employee, then no amount is due for the cost of telecommunications and equipme
perturbation.justification: The original text uses "best commercial efforts," a defined commitment. The changed text replaces this with "endeavor to promote" and introduces "potentially including, but not limited to," weakening the obligation and making responsibilities non-mandatory. The Law Explanation mentions that 'endeavor' is a vague promise. This directly contradicts the initial commitment, creating ambiguity and uncertainty about the distributor's responsibilities. The change undermines the purpose of the agreement. Since no snippet has information to provide, the LLM explanation and the change in text is enough to show a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.9. Distributor shall demonstrate the Products at the following international gaming exhibitions, ICE (London), G2E (Las Vegas), SAGSE (Buenos Aires), EELEX (Moscow) and AGE (Sydney), or any successors or equivalents to the aforementioned exhibitions. Distributor may also demonstrate the products at other gaming exhibitions where Distributor decides to exhibit, subject to there being a good business case to do so; and
perturbation.changed_text: 3.9. Distributor should consider demonstrating the Products at international gaming exhibitions, if feasible. Distributor might also demonstrate the products at other gaming exhibitions where Distributor finds it appropriate;
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall demonstrate" to "should consider demonstrating" transforms a mandatory obligation into a suggestion. Removing the list of specific exhibitions and the condition of a "good business case" further weakens the obligation, giving the Distributor complete discretion over whether to demonstrate the Products at any exhibitions. This contradicts the implied duty to use 'best commercial efforts' mentioned earlier in the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Ontario Law - implied duty of good faith in contractual performance
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Ontario law, all contracts have an implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. The original contract lists specific gaming exhibitions that the distributor *shall* attend. However, the modification introduces uncertainty over whether the distributor has any minimum performance expectation regarding the promotion and sales of Vendor's product. By reducing the duty to attend and promote at expos, it reduces the 'commercial efforts' that are expected from the distributor.

perturbation.justification: The change from "shall demonstrate" to "should consider demonstrating" weakens the obligation to promote the product at gaming exhibitions. The Perturbation Explanation states that this gives the Distributor complete discretion. The Law Explanation suggests that it reduces the 'commercial efforts' expected from the distributor, contradicting the implied duty of good faith. While the provided snippets do not directly address this specific scenario, the essence of good faith and fair dealing requires reasonable effort, which the modified text undermines. The original text provided specific exhibitions, whereas the modified text makes attendance optional, which directly reduces the expectation for the distributor to promote the product, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 14.3. Maintenance of Confidentiality. Distributor agrees that it shall take all commercially reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information. Without limiting the foregoing, Distributor shall take at least those measures that it takes to protect its own most highly confidential information and shall ensure that its employees who have access to Confidential Information have signed a non-use and non-disclosure agreement in content similar to the provisions hereof, prior to any disclosure of Confidential Information to such employees. Distributor shall not make any copies of the Confidential Information unless the same is previously approved in writing by Vendor.
perturbation.changed_text: 14.3. Maintenance of Confidentiality. Distributor agrees that it should consider taking reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information. Distributor may take measures similar to those it takes to protect its own confidential information. Distributor will encourage its employees who have access to Confidential Information to sign a non-use and non-disclosure agreement. Distributor may make copies of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification significantly weakens the Distributor's obligation to protect Confidential Information. Replacing "shall take all commercially reasonable measures" with "should consider taking reasonable measures" reduces the obligation to a suggestion. Changing "shall ensure that its employees...have signed" to "will encourage its employees...to sign" makes the signing of NDAs non-mandatory. Removing the requirement for prior written approval for copies allows the Distributor to copy the information without Vendor's consent. This can expose the Vendor to risks. A confidentiality agreement has the express purpose of keeping information as secret and restricted as possible.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Ontario Law - implied duty of good faith in contractual performance
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied duty of good faith requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance of their contractual obligations. In this context, that means actively and diligently protecting the confidential information of the other party. By drastically weakening the confidentiality obligations of the distributor, the modified section creates the opportunity for confidential information to be leaked, used improperly, or improperly copied, and demonstrates bad faith. The modification gives the distributor free-reign to copy, use, and access the Vendor's information with little-to-no repercussion.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates specific actions to protect confidential information, while the changed version weakens these obligations to suggestions and options. The original text states the distributor "shall take all commercially reasonable measures," whereas the changed text says the distributor "should consider taking reasonable measures." Similarly, the original "shall ensure that its employees...have signed" is changed to "will encourage its employees...to sign." Additionally, the distributor originally could not make copies of the confidential information without prior written approval from the vendor, but now "may make copies." Although the scraped snippets are not directly relevant to this contradiction, the Law Explanation supports that the weakened obligations demonstrate bad faith by creating the opportunity for improper use and leak of confidential information. The change removes core obligations, establishing a direct and impactful contradiction.
